title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Nature's Larder: The Revival of Wild Food Foraging
content.text: On a damp October morning in the New Forest, Robin Harford is crouching beneath an oak tree, pointing out the difference between a penny bun mushroom and its toxic lookalike. He has been leading foraging walks for fifteen years, and his knowledge of edible wild plants is encyclopaedic. (37) [37] Su interés por la recolección comenzó de forma inesperada. [/37] 'I came to it through illness,' he explains. 'I was looking for ways to reconnect with the land, and foraging just opened everything up.' || Wild food has sustained human populations for most of our species' history. It was only with the agricultural revolution, roughly ten thousand years ago, that cultivated crops began to replace gathered food as the staple diet. (38) [38] La agricultura redujo pero no eliminó la recolección de alimentos silvestres. [/38] In many rural communities, gathering mushrooms, berries and herbs continued as a supplement to farming — a tradition that survived, barely, into the twentieth century. || Interest in foraging has surged dramatically in recent years, driven by a combination of factors. Celebrity chefs have played their part, placing foraged ingredients — wood sorrel, sea purslane, wild garlic — at the centre of fashionable restaurant menus. (39) [39] Los chefs de alto perfil popularizaron los ingredientes silvestres. [/39] At the same time, growing concern about food miles, pesticide use and biodiversity loss has prompted many people to seek a more direct relationship with the food they eat. || Safety is a central concern that all responsible foragers emphasise. Several species of mushroom common in British woodland are dangerously toxic, and beginners should never eat anything they cannot identify with absolute certainty. (40) [40] La identificación errónea puede tener consecuencias graves. [/40] Harford is emphatic on this point. 'Never forage alone for mushrooms as a beginner,' he warns. 'Go with someone experienced, or join a guided walk.' || Legal questions also surround foraging. In England and Wales, picking wild plants for personal consumption on public land is generally permitted under the Countryside Act. (41) [41] Sin embargo, existen restricciones importantes que hay que respetar. [/41] Digging up roots, picking on private land without permission, and taking plants in protected areas are all prohibited — rules that responsible foragers are careful to observe. || Harford sees foraging as more than a way to gather food. (42) [42] Para él, la recolección es una forma de conexión más profunda con la naturaleza. [/42] 'When you start recognising plants,' he says, 'the landscape stops being a backdrop and becomes a place you actually inhabit.'

paragraphs.A: Their influence transformed foraged food from a rustic curiosity into a symbol of culinary sophistication.
paragraphs.B: However, taking excessive quantities or removing entire plants can cause lasting damage to fragile ecosystems.
paragraphs.C: Confusing an edible species with a poisonous one has sent numerous foragers to hospital each year.
paragraphs.D: It brought him not just nutritious food but also a profound sense of belonging to the natural world.
paragraphs.E: Yet gathering never entirely disappeared as a practice, even as farmland covered more and more of the countryside.
paragraphs.F: Collecting certain protected species or large quantities of any plant for commercial sale requires specific licences.
paragraphs.G: He did not grow up in the countryside, and his path to foraging was not a straightforward one.

questions.explanation: G introduces Harford's unexpected background before he explains his arrival at foraging through illness.
E softens the previous assertion: agriculture reduced foraging but did not eliminate it, introducing the next paragraph about its survival.
A explains the effect of celebrity chefs on the image of wild foods before the shift in focus to environmental concern.
C provides concrete evidence of the danger of misidentification before Harford's direct advice.
F specifies additional restrictions (protected species, commercial sale) that complete the legal prohibitions mentioned.
D expresses the deeper meaning Harford gives to foraging, preparing the final reflection on inhabiting the landscape.
